title: Whether failed projects deserve their own archives
summary: Argue whether preserving the records of projects that were abandoned, cancelled, or never completed serves a genuine intellectual or historical purpose.
topic: Whether failed projects deserve their own archives
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on whether failed, abandoned, or cancelled projects — buildings never built, drugs that didn't work, books left unfinished — deserve dedicated archival preservation. Refer to at least one concrete example (e.g. the Library of Lost Books at Boekenheim in Frankfurt, which holds manuscripts abandoned by their authors, or NASA's archive of cancelled mission designs). Use a cleft sentence, a concession, a participle clause, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What the Library of Lost Books at Boekenheim preserves is not a collection of literary failures but a record of…
Granted, the argument that archives should focus on what succeeded rests on a reasonable…
Having abandoned its Saturn V successor designs after the Apollo programme ended, NASA stored the blueprints in…
A failed clinical trial in which a drug proved ineffective is not merely a dead end — it is…

bị xóa sổ / bị coi là vô dụng

được khám phá lại

sampleText:
In the Boekenheim district of Frankfurt, the Library of Lost Books holds manuscripts abandoned by their authors — novels halted at chapter three, academic monographs dissolved when their central argument collapsed, memoirs left unfinished when the writer could no longer bear to proceed. The institution attracts a particular kind of visitor: researchers who want to know not only what was finished but what was attempted and stopped, and why. What the Library of Lost Books preserves is not a monument to failure but a cartography of the possible: a map of paths that were tried, found impassable, and left marked for whoever comes next.

Having cancelled its planned successor to the Saturn V rocket after the Apollo programme ended in 1972, NASA stored the unused engineering blueprints in a warehouse in Huntsville, Alabama. Forty years later, engineers developing the Space Launch System retrieved those drawings and found that several solutions to problems they were encountering had already been worked out. Without the archive of a cancelled programme, the same problems would have needed to be solved from scratch.

Granted, the argument for archiving only what succeeded has a practical logic. Storage costs money, and the effort required to catalogue and contextualise failed projects can divert resources from living work. There is also the risk that an archive of failures becomes a comfort blanket for institutions unwilling to try again: a reason to say 'it has been tried' rather than 'it should be tried better'.

Even so, the case for preserving what failed rests on two foundations that practical objections cannot easily dislodge. The first is the problem of publication bias: in medicine, thousands of clinical trials producing negative results are never published, which means that the same ineffective compounds are tested again and again by researchers who have no way of knowing the ground has already been covered. The second is the counterfactual value of abandoned work — the recognition that a project stopped does not mean a project without merit, but a project whose moment had not yet arrived.

What is lost when failure is written out of history is not merely a set of discarded files but the honesty of the record. A civilisation that archives only its successes misrepresents itself to the future — and misrepresents the future to itself. The Library of Lost Books is not a museum of regret but a library in the fullest sense: a place where what has been thought is kept retrievable, even when what was thought turned out to be wrong.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: What-cleft to reframe what an archive actually is
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What the Library of Lost Books preserves is not a monument to failure but a cartography of the possible' — cấu trúc what-cleft bác bỏ cách đọc sai ('không phải đài tưởng niệm cho sự thất bại') và thay thế bằng một hình ảnh ẩn dụ mạnh mẽ hơn ('cartography of the possible') ở vị trí cuối câu nhấn mạnh nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What the Library of Lost Books preserves is not X but Y' rejects the obvious framing and substitutes a richer metaphor at sentence-final position, making it the most memorable phrase in the paragraph.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Perfect participle clause compressing institutional history
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Having cancelled its planned successor to the Saturn V rocket after the Apollo programme ended in 1972, NASA stored the unused engineering blueprints …' — cụm phân từ hoàn thành nén một sự kiện lịch sử dài vào đầu câu, cho phép hậu quả ('stored the blueprints') theo sau một cách tự nhiên mà không cần mệnh đề quan hệ dài hơn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Having cancelled … after the Apollo programme ended, NASA stored …' compresses a prior institutional decision into a perfect participle clause, allowing the consequence to follow cleanly without a longer relative clause.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tại khu Boekenheim của Frankfurt, Thư viện Sách Thất lạc nắm giữ các bản thảo bị tác giả bỏ dở — tiểu thuyết bị dừng lại ở chương ba, các chuyên khảo học thuật tan vỡ khi lập luận trung tâm sụp đổ, hồi ký bị bỏ dở khi tác giả không còn có thể chịu đựng để tiếp tục. Tổ chức này thu hút một loại khách thăm quan đặc biệt: những nhà nghiên cứu muốn biết không chỉ những gì đã hoàn thành mà còn những gì đã được thử và dừng lại, và tại sao. Điều mà Thư viện Sách Thất lạc bảo tồn không phải là đài tưởng niệm cho sự thất bại mà là bản đồ địa lý của những điều có thể: bản đồ của những con đường đã được thử, được phát hiện không thể đi qua, và được đánh dấu lại cho người đến sau.

Sau khi đã hủy bỏ kế hoạch kế nhiệm tên lửa Saturn V sau khi chương trình Apollo kết thúc vào năm 1972, NASA đã lưu trữ các bản thiết kế kỹ thuật không được sử dụng trong một kho hàng ở Huntsville, Alabama. Bốn mươi năm sau, các kỹ sư đang phát triển Hệ thống Phóng không gian đã lấy lại những bản vẽ đó và phát hiện rằng một số giải pháp cho những vấn đề họ đang gặp phải đã được giải quyết rồi. Nếu không có kho lưu trữ của một chương trình bị hủy, những vấn đề tương tự sẽ cần phải được giải quyết từ đầu.

Công nhận, lập luận chỉ lưu trữ những gì thành công có một logic thực tế. Lưu trữ tốn tiền, và nỗ lực cần thiết để lập danh mục và đặt ngữ cảnh cho các dự án thất bại có thể chuyển hướng nguồn lực khỏi công việc đang sống. Cũng có nguy cơ rằng một kho lưu trữ các thất bại trở thành tấm chăn an ủi cho các tổ chức không muốn thử lại: một lý do để nói 'nó đã được thử' thay vì 'nó nên được thử tốt hơn'.

Dẫu vậy, lập luận cho việc bảo tồn những gì thất bại dựa trên hai nền tảng mà các phản đối thực tế không thể dễ dàng gạt bỏ. Thứ nhất là vấn đề về thiên kiến xuất bản: trong y học, hàng nghìn thử nghiệm lâm sàng tạo ra kết quả âm tính không bao giờ được công bố, có nghĩa là cùng những hợp chất không hiệu quả được thử nghiệm lại và lại bởi các nhà nghiên cứu không có cách nào biết rằng mảnh đất đó đã được khai phá rồi. Thứ hai là giá trị phản thực tế của công việc bị bỏ dở — sự thừa nhận rằng một dự án bị dừng lại không có nghĩa là một dự án không có giá trị, mà là một dự án mà thời điểm của nó chưa đến.

Điều bị mất khi sự thất bại bị xóa khỏi lịch sử không chỉ là một tập hợp các tệp bị loại bỏ mà là sự trung thực của hồ sơ. Một nền văn minh chỉ lưu trữ những thành công của mình đã bóp méo hình ảnh của chính mình với tương lai — và bóp méo tương lai với chính mình. Thư viện Sách Thất lạc không phải là bảo tàng của sự hối tiếc mà là thư viện theo nghĩa đầy đủ nhất: nơi những gì đã được nghĩ đến được giữ có thể truy xuất, ngay cả khi những gì đã được nghĩ đến hóa ra là sai.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to reframe what the Library of Lost Books actually preserves?
options: What the Library of Lost Books preserves is not a monument to failure but a cartography of the possible.
The Library of Lost Books preserves what is not a monument to failure but a cartography.
It is the Library of Lost Books that preserves what failure and the possible cartography.
What preserves the Library of Lost Books is not failure but possible cartography.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X preserves is not Y but Z' uses a what-cleft to reframe the institution: rejecting the 'monument to failure' reading and replacing it with 'cartography of the possible'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the pattern 'What X preserves is not … but …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a perfect participle clause to compress a prior institutional decision?
options: Having cancelled its planned successor to the Saturn V rocket after the Apollo programme ended, NASA stored the unused engineering blueprints in a warehouse.
NASA having cancelled the Saturn V successor, the blueprints were stored in a warehouse after Apollo.
Cancelling the Saturn V successor, NASA is storing the blueprints after the Apollo programme.
After the Apollo programme ending, NASA having cancelled stored the blueprints in a warehouse.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having cancelled …, NASA stored …' is a correct perfect participle clause: the participle clause shares its subject (NASA) with the main clause, and the action precedes the main event.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Having + past participle' as the opener, same subject as the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the Library of Lost Books at Boekenheim, as described in the essay?
options: A collection of books that were banned by their governments
A digital archive of out-of-print books
An institution holding manuscripts abandoned by their authors for various reasons
A lending library for books that could not find a publisher
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes it as holding 'manuscripts abandoned by their authors — novels halted at chapter three, academic monographs dissolved when their central argument collapsed, memoirs left unfinished'.
mcQuestions.hint: Re-read the first sentence of the essay.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the NASA archive example demonstrate?
options: That NASA should have completed the Apollo programme
That cancelled projects can contain solutions to future problems
That warehouse storage is cheaper than digital archiving
That engineers in the 1970s were more skilled than those today
mcQuestions.explanation: The engineers developing the Space Launch System found that 'several solutions to problems they were encountering had already been worked out' in the cancelled Saturn V successor blueprints.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at what the engineers found when they retrieved the old drawings.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'publication bias' mean as used in the essay?
options: The tendency for publishers to prefer books by famous authors
The tendency for only positive results to be published, while negative results go unreported
The tendency for archives to preserve only published books
The preference for digital publications over print ones
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines the problem as: 'thousands of clinical trials producing negative results are never published', causing the same failed compounds to be tested repeatedly.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay explains the problem directly — look for the word 'never published'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly states that the same failed compounds keep being tested?
options: the same ineffective compounds are tested again and again
again and again the compounds test ineffective the same
the same compounds are ineffective testing again
tested again the same ineffective compounds are and again
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Are tested again and again' is the correct present passive; the agent (researchers) is omitted to keep focus on the compounds being repeatedly subjected to the same tests.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'counterfactual value' mean in the context of abandoned projects?
options: The monetary value a project would have had if completed
The insight gained from knowing what a project revealed about what was not possible at the time
The negative publicity that comes from publicising failures
The legal value of unfinished contracts
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'counterfactual value' to mean the worth of knowing that a project was stopped not because it was without merit but because its moment had not arrived — informing future attempts.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Counterfactual' refers to what could have been, or what a different path might have produced.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which concession does the essay make in favour of not archiving failures?
options: Failures are morally embarrassing and should be kept private.
Archiving failures costs money and could become a reason to avoid trying again.
Failures are less interesting to researchers than successes.
Most failed projects contain no useful information.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concedes that 'storage costs money' and that an archive of failures could become 'a comfort blanket … a reason to say it has been tried rather than it should be tried better'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the third paragraph, which begins with 'Granted'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'a project whose moment had not yet arrived'?
options: A project that was delivered late
A project that was ahead of its time and could succeed in a different context
A project that never had any realistic chance of success
A project that was too expensive to complete at the time
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that an abandoned project may simply have been ahead of its time — its conditions for success hadn't yet developed, not that it was without value.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about historical inventions that were abandoned but later succeeded.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase from the essay means 'a detailed map of what was possible to attempt'?
options: institutional memory
cartography of the possible
the honesty of the record
a comfort blanket
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Cartography of the possible' is a metaphor for a map of all the paths that were tried — including those that led nowhere — allowing future travellers to know where others have already been.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Cartography' means the practice of making maps.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay identify as the deepest loss when failure is excluded from historical records?
options: The loss of interesting stories for future historians
The loss of the honesty of the record
The loss of storage space in warehouses
The loss of funding for future projects
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'What is lost when failure is written out of history is not merely a set of discarded files but the honesty of the record.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the what-cleft in the fifth paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which opening sentence best grounds the essay in a specific, named institution?
options: In today's world, archiving is an important topic that affects many institutions.
It is widely believed that failed projects should be preserved for future generations.
In the Boekenheim district of Frankfurt, the Library of Lost Books holds manuscripts abandoned by their authors.
Throughout history, human beings have debated whether failure should be remembered.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C opens with a specific district, a specific city, a specific institution, and a specific collection — no generic framing needed.
mcQuestions.hint: District + city + institution + collection beats every generic opener.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition introduces the pivot back to the main argument after the concession?
options: For instance,
Moreover,
Even so,
That is to say,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the conceded points (cost, risk of comfort blanket) while reasserting that the case for preserving failure still stands.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Even so' = 'despite those valid points, here is why my argument holds'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to duplicate effort' mean in the context of publication bias?
options: To make two copies of the same document
To unknowingly repeat work that has already been done and found to fail
To publish the same study twice in different journals
To apply for the same grant twice
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes researchers testing the same ineffective compounds because they have no way of knowing others have already tried and failed — this is duplicated effort.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what happens when you don't know what has already been attempted.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains a formal B2 register?
options: Failed projects are actually pretty interesting and we should def keep them in archives.
Like, it's obvious that throwing away failed work is a bad idea tbh.
A civilisation that archives only its successes misrepresents itself to the future — and misrepresents the future to itself.
Keeping failed projects is important but storage is expensive so it's complicated.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses precise vocabulary ('civilisation', 'archives', 'misrepresents') and a rhetorical chiasmus that demonstrates sophisticated command of written English.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'def', 'tbh', 'actually pretty', and hedging informal phrases.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay's final description of the Library of Lost Books differ from how it might be negatively characterised?
options: The essay calls it a tourist attraction rather than a research institution.
The essay calls it a library in the fullest sense, not a museum of regret.
The essay calls it a monument to failure, not a practical resource.
The essay calls it a comfort blanket for failing authors.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's final sentence explicitly contrasts 'a museum of regret' (the negative reading) with 'a library in the fullest sense' (the essay's reframing), echoing the opening what-cleft.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the contrast in the very last sentence.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever abandoned a project — a piece of writing, a plan, a skill you were learning? If someone preserved a record of that abandoned work, what would they learn from it that your finished work would not tell them?
